settings.title: Read These Three Clauses Before You Sign
settings.excerpt: A short guide to the contract terms that cause the most trouble long after the ink is dry.
settings.body:
Most commercial disputes we see do not come from bad faith. They come from terms nobody read closely at the start. A few clauses cause more than their share of problems.

Termination. Know how either side can end the agreement, how much notice is required, and what happens to work already in progress. Many arguments come down to one party assuming they could walk away cleanly when the contract said otherwise.

Liability. Caps and exclusions decide who carries the cost when something goes wrong, and they are often buried near the end. A single sentence here can be the difference between a manageable problem and one that threatens the business.

Governing law. This decides where and under whose rules any dispute is settled, which matters enormously if the other party is elsewhere.

How to read a contract before you sign it

You do not need legal training to give an agreement a sensible first pass. A calm read with a pen in hand catches most of the terms that cause trouble later. The trick is to look for the parts that decide what happens when things do not go to plan, rather than the parts that describe the happy path.

  • Mark any clause you do not fully understand
  • Note every number, especially dates, notice periods, and money
  • Check what happens if either side wants to leave early

When to call us

None of this replaces proper advice, but knowing where to look turns a contract from a mystery into a document you can question. If a deal is large, runs for a long time, or matters to the future of the business, that is the moment to have a second set of eyes on it before you sign rather than after.
